figures.gloss: The high creator-god of the Meccan haram — 'the God', maker of heaven and earth whom the pagans themselves invoked in extremity, yet who kept his distance behind a screen of intercessor-deities and daughters.
figures.facet: The shard caught mid-withdrawal: a supreme creator already acknowledged as highest, but worshipped through go-betweens — the deus otiosus on the very eve of being declared the only God.
figures.provenance: Attested in the Qur'an's own polemic (e.g. Q 29:61, 39:38) and in pre-Islamic theophoric names ('Abd Allah). The continuity of the name into Islam is historical fact; the pre-Islamic theology is reconstructed from later sources.

figures.gloss: The chief idol of the Kaaba, a red-carnelian image whose seven divining arrows were cast for the great decisions of the Quraysh — the presiding lord of Mecca's sanctuary and its oracle.
figures.facet: Where the high-god is also the diviner: Hubal does not merely rule the shrine but ANSWERS through it, the sky-lord as the oracle whose arrows decide marriage, burial and war.
figures.provenance: Ibn al-Kalbi's Book of Idols and Ibn Hisham's Sira. Possibly of Nabataean/Aramaic origin; the lunar association is inferred, contested.

figures.gloss: 'The Goddess' — a celestial, sun-bright mother venerated from Ta'if to Palmyra, seated upon a great white stone, one of the three 'daughters of Allah' and among the most widely worshipped powers of Arabia.
figures.facet: The feminine sun: where elsewhere the solar eye is male, al-Lat carries the brightness of the sky as a goddess, the mother-power of the celestial day.
figures.provenance: Book of Idols; Palmyrene and Nabataean inscriptions. Herodotus equated her with Alilat/Aphrodite-Urania. The solar reading is one strand among several (some sources lunar/Venus).

figures.gloss: 'The Mightiest One' — the morning-star goddess of the Quraysh, worshipped in a grove of acacias at Nakhla and honoured with blood, the second of Allah's three daughters and the dread power of Venus.
figures.facet: The shard where Venus is terror and might rather than mere love: al-Uzza receives human sacrifice, the morning star as a goddess to be feared, not only desired.
figures.provenance: Book of Idols; the 'Satanic Verses' episode names her with al-Lat and Manat. Identified with the planet Venus; tier-2 kinship with the wider Inanna/Ishtar/Astarte Venus complex.

figures.gloss: The oldest of the three goddesses, lady of fate, fortune and the appointed term — she who measures out destiny and death, worshipped at a black stone on the shore between Mecca and Medina by the Aws and Khazraj.
figures.facet: The divine apportioner of doom: not a queen of the dead but the goddess who allots WHEN each life ends — fate and mortality fused into a single feminine power, her very name from 'manaya', the death-fates.
figures.provenance: Book of Idols; her name shares a root with 'maniyya' (allotted death) and may echo the Greek Moira / Roman Fortuna function. Reconstructed; pre-Islamic theology incompletely documented.

figures.gloss: The moon-god and national deity of the Sabaeans of Saba, lord of the great temple at Marib (Awwam) — the bull-horned patron whose people called themselves 'the children of Almaqah'.
figures.facet: The South-Arabian inversion of the Mediterranean sky: here the MOON is the supreme state-god and patriarch, the lunar power raised to the place a sun- or sky-father holds elsewhere.
figures.provenance: Sabaean inscriptions; the Awwam (Mahram Bilqis) temple at Marib. The bull/lunar symbolism is epigraphically attested; precise gender-of-moon conventions vary across South Arabia.

figures.gloss: The moon-god of the Hadramawt and the broader South-Arabian triad of Moon, Sun and Venus — reckoner of the calendar and the cycle, the night-light that orders the agricultural and ritual year.
figures.facet: Time's own measurer: where the lunar god is above all the COUNTER of months and seasons, the celestial clock by which a desert civilisation kept its appointments with the gods.
figures.provenance: South-Arabian inscriptions; named in common with Mesopotamian Sin of Ur/Harran, with whom there is real cultic kinship (tier-2 contact), not mere coincidence of name.

figures.gloss: The cube-shrine at the navel of Arabia and its embedded black betyl — a sacred stone circumambulated by pilgrims, the centre of the haram where the idols of the tribes were gathered and the rites of the hajj performed.
figures.facet: The aniconic centre: a holy STONE rather than an image, the fallen-from-heaven betyl as the still point of the turning world — a quartered-cosmos sanctuary that survived its own gods' abolition.
figures.provenance: Pre-Islamic betyl/stone-cult is well attested across the Semitic Near East (massebah, Nabataean betyls). The Kaaba's pre-Islamic pilgrimage role is recorded in the Sira and Islamic tradition.

figures.gloss: 'Lord of the Mountain (Shara)' — the chief god of the Nabataeans of Petra, worshipped as a great unhewn block of stone, a mountain-and-vegetation lord whom the Greeks read as a Dionysos of the desert.
figures.facet: The god who IS the stone: the most uncompromising betyl-deity, a high-god represented by a raw quadrangular rock — divinity as the unworked mountain itself.
figures.provenance: Nabataean inscriptions; classical sources (Tertullian, the Suda) equate Dushara with Dionysos, hence the vegetation/dying-rising strand. The dying-rising reading is an interpretatio graeca, tier-4.

edges.note: Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A transcendent, singular absolute whose primary interaction with humanity is through a captured, dictated word or voice.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The absolute, sealed physical center of the faith, strictly restricted to the ritually pure and initiated.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A fascinating inversion: orthodox Islam's supreme deity is structurally analogous to Alawism's secondary emanation, as both are the active source of exoteric revelation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An iconic, non-human stone monolith serving as the absolute geographic and spiritual focal point of a massive pilgrimage network.
Within Arabia: al-Lat, al-Uzza and Manat are named in the tradition as the 'daughters of Allah' (banat Allah), the intercessor-goddesses through whom the withdrawn high-god was approached.
The second 'daughter of Allah': al-Uzza of the Quraysh stands with al-Lat and Manat as the trio of goddesses mediating to the high creator-god.
The eldest 'daughter of Allah': Manat completes the triad of goddesses the Meccan polemic singles out as the false intercessors beside the one God.
The two brightest of the three goddesses, repeatedly invoked together (al-Lat the celestial mother, al-Uzza the Venus-power) at the heart of Quraysh worship.
Hubal stood as the presiding idol within the Kaaba and his divining arrows were cast there — the chief god and the axis-shrine were a single cultic centre.
Both are South-Arabian moon-gods heading regional triads of Moon, Sun and Venus; Almaqah leads Saba, Sin the Hadramawt — variant lunar high-gods of the same astral pattern.
Fate and Venus among the three daughters: Manat allots the term of death, al-Uzza commands might and the morning star — the apportioner beside the dread goddess.

The defining historical adjacency of the mirror: 'Allah' (al-ilah, 'the God') is the same Semitic high-god word as Hebrew 'El/Eloah', and the Meccan supreme creator was reidentified as the one God of Abraham — pagan high-god to monotheist absolute by documented continuity.

The Arabian betyl-cult of sacred standing stones is cousin to the Canaanite/Israelite massebah and the stone of Bethel ('house of God') Jacob anointed — a shared Semitic holy-stone practice, the same family of aniconic centre.
Both are West-Semitic high-gods named by title rather than name ('the God' / 'the Lord, Baal'); the Arabian and Canaanite supreme deities share a Semitic theological grammar of lordship.

The Arabian morning-star goddess belongs to the same Near-Eastern Venus complex as Inanna/Ishtar and Astarte — feared, blood-receiving, identified with the planet; real cultic kinship across Semitic worlds, no direct descent claimed.

Both great celestial mother-goddesses of the late ancient world, each absorbing other goddesses into a near-universal feminine power; al-Lat was equated by Greeks with Aphrodite-Urania, Isis with many — sisters of the syncretic Great Mother.
Both are feminine powers of fate and death, allotting the doom of warriors — Manat measures the appointed term as the Morrigan presides over slaughter; the goddess as apportioner of mortality.
Two feminine powers bound to death, but where Hel rules the dead in their place, Manat decides the MOMENT of dying — the underworld-queen and the fate-allotter as complementary faces of mortality.
The moon as reckoner of time and calendar — a function shared with lunar Thoth and others; the night-light as the cosmos's measurer. (No Thoth id listed; seam noted via the lunar joint.)
The hinge of which luminary rules: South Arabia raises the MOON to national high-god while Shinto raises the SUN to imperial ancestor — the same supreme-luminary office filled by opposite lights.
Two rare solar GODDESSES against the world's usual male sun: al-Lat as the sun-bright celestial mother and Amaterasu as the heavenly radiance — the feminine sun appearing independently in two shards.
Both presiding high-gods of a great sanctuary who answer through oracle and decree, though Hubal speaks by divining arrows rather than the solar barque — the shrine-lord as oracle.
Both are the sacred centre of the world, the navel where heaven meets earth — the Kaaba as omphalos-shrine rhyming with the quartered garden at the cosmos's heart.
The axis-mundi shared: a still central point the faithful orient toward and circumambulate, as cosmologies the world over fix their centre on a holy mountain or stone.

Classical writers (Tertullian, the Suda) explicitly identified Nabataean Dushara with Dionysos — a documented interpretatio graeca that grafted vine, mystery and the dying-rising strand onto the desert mountain-god.

Within the Semitic west: Dushara worshipped as a raw quadrangular block and the Kaaba's betyl are the same betyl/massebah stone-cult — sacred unhewn stone as the dwelling of the god, a shared Arabian practice (functional kinship, not a sound-law cognate).

The South-Arabian moon-cult shares real Near-Eastern lineage with Mesopotamian lunar worship; the Hadrami moon-god even bears the name Sin of Ur and Harran — documented cultic continuity across the Semitic world.
GOLD SEAM: Arising from an Islamic context, the Bahá'í Faith explicitly identifies the Essence of God with Allah, maintaining strict Abrahamic monotheism.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A loose parallel driven by modern politics; Acintya's absolute singularity was heavily emphasized to align with the Islamic-influenced Indonesian state ideology of Pancasila.
GOLD SEAM: Supreme oracle gods presiding over harsh desert environments, communicating truth through aniconic objects or divination rather than inscribed texts.
GOLD SEAM: Warrior gods of nomadic and semi-nomadic tribes, represented in battle and sanctuary by unhewn stones or aniconic idols rather than human statues.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Allah is explicitly claimed in Caodaist texts as one of the Father's prior divine identities.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The veneration of a dark, heaven-sent meteorite as the supreme, aniconic terrestrial anchor for the divine presence.
GOLD SEAM: Emerging from an Ismaili Islamic context, Al-Hakim is viewed by the Druze as the ultimate physical manifestation of the one God, Allah.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A singular, sacred physical object that acts as the absolute focal point of binding spiritual and communal gravity.
GOLD SEAM: Universally recognized, rigidly non-anthropomorphic focal points that anchor the shared identity and law of a vast, disparate trade network.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both serve as the mandatory physical qibla, anchoring community ritual through orientation to a supreme sacred center.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Sabians explicitly equated their transcendent First Cause with Allah to secure protected dhimmi status under Islamic rule.
GOLD SEAM: Muhammad revolutionized the pre-Islamic High God of the Quraysh by stripping away all associated partner deities to assert strict monotheism.
GOLD SEAM: The literal integration of the pre-Islamic Meccan sacred center and its meteorite into the heart of the new monotheistic framework.

GOLD SEAM: Both are the sole creator, sovereign, and lawgiver of the universe, revealed through scripture and covenant — but YHWH is unpronounceable and personal, while Allah is the generic name for God in Arabic. The two traditions share a common ancestor in the Abrahamic line but diverged radically.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: In modern Chitral, the Kalash often explicitly identify their supreme creator Dezau with Allah (Khodai) to maintain coexistence.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Explicitly merged with the Islamic concept of Tawhid by the Wali Songo (Nine Saints) to Islamize the Javanese interior.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An aniconic, dark stone serving as the absolute, physical focal point of divine presence, radically centralizing unmediated worship.
GOLD SEAM: Universally recognized, rigidly non-anthropomorphic focal points that anchor the shared identity and ritual geography of a civilization.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Explicitly adopts the Islamic name and ultimate authority of God, but radically reimagines him as a living Black man.
GOLD SEAM: Pre-eminent celestial goddesses of Semitic trading networks, often venerated through aniconic stones or abstract symbols rather than human statuary.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both enforce a radical, indivisible monotheism that strictly rejects any partners, emanations, or divine incarnations.

Sacred stones recognised as holy and made the focus of offering and pilgrimage; the Sámi offering-rock and the Black Stone are far-flung, unrelated answers to the impulse to find godhead lodged in stone — a distant rhyme, flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Historically, Islamic Serer populations have mapped Roog directly onto Allah to harmonize indigenous theology with Islam.
GOLD SEAM: The Gurus frequently used Islamic names for God ('Allah', 'Khuda') interchangeably with Hindu names, asserting the absolute identity of the one formless creator worshipped by both.
GOLD SEAM: A singular, physical object at the center of the faith that serves as the ultimate earthly focal point, vehemently distinguished from an idol, yet treated with supreme physical reverence.
GOLD SEAM: Both represent the aniconic worship of a sacred black stone (baetyl) with Semitic/Near Eastern origins, embodying the divine presence on earth without human form.
GOLD SEAM: Both are Near Eastern deities worshipped in the form of unhewn stone blocks or meteorites, later given Hellenized/Romanized solar or sovereign attributes.
GOLD SEAM: A stark, cuboid stone sanctuary serving as the absolute, aniconic focal point of divine presence on earth.
GOLD SEAM: Xwedê shares linguistic and regional history with the Islamic Allah, representing absolute, unpartnered monotheism.

dossier.essence: The polytheisms of the Arabian Peninsula before Islam — the tribal cults of the north and center (Mecca's Hubal, al-Lat, al-Uzza, and Manat, with Allah as high creator), the temple-states of South Arabia (Saba's national moon-god Almaqah and kin), and Nabataean Petra (Dushara). As public religion it ended with the destruction of the Kaaba idols in 630 CE, surviving as substrate within Islam and in tens of thousands of inscriptions.
dossier.cosmos: No systematic native cosmology survives; what is attested is a high creator invoked in extremity but approached through intercessor deities, prominent astral worship in the south, and a world shared with jinn. Pre-Islamic poetry is strikingly fatalistic — dahr, impersonal Time, devours all — and evidence for afterlife hope is thin; the Qur'an's own polemic records Meccan denial of resurrection. Fate-goddesses such as Manat personified the allotted term of life.
dossier.practice: Cult centered on sanctuaries (haram) with sacred stones (betyls) as often as images: pilgrimage, circumambulation, animal sacrifice, votive offerings, and divination by arrows before Hubal, while seers (kahins) delivered oracles in rhymed prose. Sacred truce months made pilgrimage and markets possible across tribal war; South Arabian temples ran formal economies with dedications, tithes, and ritual hunts.
dossier.canon: No scripture: the sources are epigraphy (Safaitic, Sabaic, Nabataean), pre-Islamic poetry, the Qur'an's polemic, and later Islamic antiquarianism — above all Ibn al-Kalbi's 'Book of Idols' (early 9th century).
dossier.reflects: The aniconic sacred center — stone, sanctuary, truce, and pilgrimage as religion's skeleton — preserved chiefly through the polemic of the tradition that replaced it.
sources: Robert G. Hoyland, 'Arabia and the Arabs' (2001)
G. R. Hawting, 'The Idea of Idolatry and the Emergence of Islam' (1999)
Ahmad Al-Jallad, 'The Religion and Rituals of the Nomads of Pre-Islamic Arabia' (2022)
Ibn al-Kalbi, 'Kitab al-Asnam (Book of Idols)' (early 9th c.)
